svg.text: SWAHILI
PHONOLOGY
SWAHILI CONSONANT SYSTEM
Stops: p b • t d • k g (no aspiration contrast)
Fricatives: f v • s z • sh /ʃ/ • gh /ɣ/ • h
Affricates: ch /tʃ/ • dh /ð/ • th /θ/ (Arabic loans)
Nasals: m n ng’ /ŋ/ • Prenasalized: mb nd ng nj
SWAHILI VOWELS
Five pure vowels: a e i o u (no long/short distinction)
No vowel reduction (every vowel pronounced fully)
Penultimate stress: almost always on second-to-last syllable
SYLLABLE STRUCTURE
Preferred: CV (consonant + vowel) • V also possible
Syllabic nasals: m-tu "person" • n-chi "country"
No final consonants in native words (all end in vowel)
KIKUYU PHONOLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
KIKUYU TONAL SYSTEM
Kikuyu is a TONAL language (Swahili traditionally is not)
High (H) and Low (L) tones distinguish meaning and grammar:
tHaaté "three" • thaate "to walk" (tone distinguishes)
Tone also marks tense in verbs: perfective vs. imperfective
KIKUYU CONSONANTS
Similar inventory to Swahili with key additions:
ɣ /ɣ/ (voiced velar fricative) • δ /ð/ (voiced dental fricative)
Prenasalized stops: mb nd ng (same as Swahili Bantu heritage)
KIKUYU VOWELS
Five vowels with phonemic length distinction: a/aa i/ii u/uu e/ee o/oo
Unlike Swahili: long vowels are phonemic in Kikuyu
mūkĩ "woman" • mūkii "old woman" (length distinction)
SHARED BANTU FEATURES
Both: 5 vowels • prenasalized stops • syllabic nasals
Both: CV syllable preference • open syllable words
Kikuyu: adds tone + vowel length • Swahili: simpler phonology
Swahili simplicity: why it spread as a trade language
PLATE 20b ALT · SWAHILI / KIKUYU · PHONOLOGY
